title: Share of deaths from suicide
subtitle: Estimated share of deaths from suicide. Suicides are underreported in many countries due to social stigma and cultural or legal concerns. This data is based on modeling of global suicide patterns, and includes adjustments and extrapolations that aim to account for missing data and underreporting.
caption:
Share of deaths from suicide

Estimated share of deaths from suicide. Suicides are underreported in many countries due to social stigma and cultural or legal concerns. This data is based on modeling of global suicide patterns, and includes adjustments and extrapolations that aim to account for missing data and underreporting.

The line chart tracks the estimated share of deaths from suicide for seven countries from 1980 to 2023, with the vertical axis ranging from 0% to over 6%. South Korea exhibits the most significant variation, starting near 2.3% in 1980, rising steadily to a peak above 6% around 2010, and subsequently declining to approximately 4% by 2023. The United States and Japan follow distinct trajectories; the United States begins around 1.5% and climbs gradually to nearly 2% by the end of the period, while Japan starts higher at roughly 3.1%, peaks near 3.6% in the mid-1980s, and trends downward to finish near 1.6%. Germany and the United Kingdom remain in the lower-middle range, generally fluctuating between 1% and 1.6% throughout the timeline, with Germany ending slightly above the United Kingdom. Peru and Greece occupy the lowest bands; Peru shows a slow increase from near 0.2% to roughly 0.7%, while Greece stays consistently below 0.5% for the entire duration. The data reflects modeled estimates intended to address underreporting issues associated with social stigma and legal concerns.
